title-group.article-title: Specialist Healthcare Intervention and Follow-up Trends in Post-Acute COVID-19 Hospitalization as Compared to Other Respiratory Infections

contrib-group.aff: 1https://ror.org/05dy5ab02grid.507997.50000 0004 5984 6051Department of Biomedical and Clinical Sciences, Infectious and Tropical Diseases Operational Unit, ASST Fatebenefratelli Sacco, “L. Sacco” University Hospital, Via Giovanni Battista Grassi, 20157 Milan, Italy

license.license-p: Open Access This article is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial 4.0 International License, which permits any non-commercial use, sharing, adaptation, distribution and reproduction in any medium or format, as long as you give appropriate credit to the original author(s) and the source, provide a link to the Creative Commons licence, and indicate if changes were made. The images or other third party material in this article are included in the article's Creative Commons licence, unless indicated otherwise in a credit line to the material. If material is not included in the article's Creative Commons licence and your intended use is not permitted by statutory regulation or exceeds the permitted use, you will need to obtain permission directly from the copyright holder. To view a copy of this licence, visit http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Post-acute sequelae of COVID-19, often referred to as “long COVID,” have raised concerns about increased healthcare utilization following hospitalization. Whether these patterns differ significantly from those observed after other acute respiratory infections (ARIs) remains unclear. This study aimed to compare post-discharge healthcare use between patients hospitalized for COVID-19 and those with other ARIs in Lombardy, Italy.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: We conducted a population-based cohort study using 2021 administrative healthcare data from the Lombardy Region. Patients aged ≥ 40 years hospitalized for COVID-19 or other ARIs were followed for 12 months post-discharge. We evaluated specialist consultations, rehospitalizations, diagnostic testing, and new chronic drug treatment initiations. Logistic regression models adjusted for age, sex, and comorbidities (Drug-Derived Complexity Index) were used to assess differences.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Among 57,795 patients, 35,458 were hospitalized for COVID-19 and 21,375 for other ARIs. Patients with COVID-19 were younger and had lower comorbidity burden and post-discharge mortality (10.7% vs. 33.5%). A higher proportion received at least one specialist visit (75.8% vs. 70.3%), though with a longer median time to first visit (63 vs. 45 days, p < 0.0001). Patients with COVID-19 had more frequent imaging and spirometry but initiated fewer chronic drug treatments overall. However, a higher prescription rate for antidiabetics (OR 1.42), psychoanaleptic (OR 1.21), and genitourinary/hormonal drugs (OR 1.29) emerged after COVID-19 hospitalizations: this rate remained statistically higher for antidiabetics even after excluding subjects who died in the year following discharge. Hospitalizations for causes other than COVID-19 were more frequent in patients with ARI.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: Compared to other ARIs, COVID-19 survivors exhibited distinct post-discharge healthcare patterns, with delayed but focused specialist care and selective increases in diagnostic and pharmacological interventions.

sec.title: Supplementary Information
sec.p: The online version contains supplementary material available at 10.1007/s40121-025-01249-5.

custom-meta.meta-value: © Springer Healthcare Ltd., part of Springer Nature 2025

tr.td: Given the uniqueness of post-COVID sequelae, we investigated whether its impacts on healthcare interventions were different from those of other acute respiratory diseases.
Subjects hospitalized in the Lombardy region for COVID-19 or another acute respiratory disease (ARI) were followed for 12 months after discharge using the regional administrative database.

bold.italic: What was learned from the study?

tr.td: During the 120-day period following hospital discharge, the incidence of specialist visits in the COVID-19 cohort was lower compared to other ARIs. However, this trend reversed thereafter, possibly indicating late emergence of post-COVID symptomatology.

People discharged after COVID-19 are less clinically complicated than those with other ARIs, have lower 1-year mortality and fewer new chronic drug therapies, with the notable exception of antidiabetics.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Following the initial acute phase of the COVID-19 pandemic, it quickly became clear that long-term sequelae of SARS-CoV-2 infection posed a significant ongoing challenge [1]. These persistent issues, known as “long COVID” or “post-acute COVID-19 syndrome” [2], encompass a range of cognitive, mental, and physical impairments. While long-term complications are not unique to COVID-19, being well-documented after other acute respiratory infections (ARIs) and various infectious conditions [3], the COVID-19 pandemic specifically heightened concerns about the potentially greater and more complex long-term healthcare needs of survivors compared to those recovering from other ARIs.

This has spurred extensive research, which generally suggests that COVID-19’s long-term sequelae might be more diverse [4], and persistent [5]. Studies have also shown an increased risk of worsening pre-existing chronic conditions or developing new ones, like cardiovascular diseases [6] and diabetes mellitus [7]. These factors, including the development of more severe or novel pathologies and the apparent increased frequency and distinct nature of COVID-19 sequelae compared to other ARIs, make it crucial to investigate their impact on healthcare systems.

Given the vast number of individuals infected during the pandemic and evidence pointing to a higher incidence and potentially unique nature of post-COVID sequelae, it is plausible that many survivors will need more specialist medical consultations. This anticipated rise in demand is likely to lead to a corresponding increase in diagnostic and therapeutic procedures, including imaging and new or intensified pharmacological treatments, putting considerable strain on healthcare systems, especially publicly funded ones like the Italian National Health Service (SSN—Servizio Sanitario Nazionale). While some research has started to quantify the increased costs associated with SARS-CoV-2 infection [8], a detailed comparative analysis of specific healthcare resource utilization, such as specialist consultations, rehospitalizations, and the initiation of new chronic treatments, across large cohorts of patients hospitalized for COVID-19 and other ARIs remains an important area for exploration. With this in mind, our study aimed to comparatively assess the post-acute healthcare burden of patients hospitalized for COVID-19 versus those hospitalized for other ARIs, using extensive administrative data from Lombardy, Northern Italy.

Specifically, the primary objective was to compare post-acute healthcare utilization, including specialist consultations, rehospitalizations, and initiation of chronic drug treatments, between patients hospitalized for COVID-19 and those hospitalized for other ARIs in Lombardy. The secondary objective was to assess differences in the timing and frequency of specialist visits across the two groups.

sec.title: Data Sources
sec.p: Hospitalization data for residents of the Lombardy region aged 40 years and older in 2021 were obtained from the regional healthcare database, originally established for administrative and reimbursement purposes. Lombardy, located in Northern Italy, has a population of approximately 10 million people.

The administrative database includes information on demographic characteristics (age and sex), hospital admissions, drug dispensations, and outpatient services [9]. Drug prescriptions reimbursed by the Italian National Health Service were classified using the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) system [10]. No information is available for dispensations during hospital stay, in nursing homes, nor for medications purchased out-of-pocket. The ATC classification divides drugs into different groups according to the target organ or system, therapeutic purposes, and chemical characteristics. For these analyses we used the first (main anatomical or pharmacological groups) and second (pharmacological or therapeutic subgroups) level of the ATC classification. Chronic drug use was defined as receiving six or more dispensations during the observation period. To estimate comorbidity, we used the Drug-Derived Complexity Index (DDCI) [11], based on drug therapy data from the year preceding hospitalization. Hospital admission records include up to six diagnoses coded according to the International Classification of Diseases, Ninth Revision, Clinical Modification (ICD-9-CM) [12]. Outpatient service records cover requests for diagnostic, therapeutic, or rehabilitative services, including laboratory and imaging procedures. The comorbidities were calculated using drug prescriptions (in the year 2020) and ICD-9-CM codes recorded during hospital discharges (in the previous 5 years). This method allows us to systematically categorize patient health statuses and track specific medical conditions, providing a robust and standardized approach to quantifying comorbidity burdens within the study cohort.

sec.title: Ethical Approval
sec.p: According to Italian law, studies using retrospective aggregated data from administrative databases that do not involve direct access by investigators to individual patient data do not require approval, notification from an ethics committee/institutional review board, or patient informed consent. Data access was granted through an agreement between the Istituto di Ricerche Farmacologiche Mario Negri IRCCS and the Lombardy Welfare Directorate. All data were not identifiable and were handled in compliance with Italian privacy regulations.

sec.title: Study Cohort and Definitions
sec.p: Starting from January 2021, the first hospitalization associated with an ARI, as defined by the diagnostic codes listed in Supplementary Table 1, was identified for each individual aged 40 years or older. Patients were included if at least one of the six diagnostic codes fell within the ARI set of interest. Specifically, Supplementary Table 1 lists the diagnoses and ICD-9-CM codes used to define both the COVID-19 and the other ARI populations included in the study. Those with both COVID-19 and another ARI diagnosis during the same hospitalization were analyzed as a distinct subgroup.

Inclusion criteria were age ≥ 40 years, alive at discharge, and not residing in nursing homes during 2021–2022 (as drug dispensation data were unavailable for these individuals). After discharge, patients were followed for a year, for the following outcomes: death, rehospitalizations, and use of outpatient specialist visits, imaging, and functional and biochemical tests.

sec.title: Statistical Analysis
sec.p: Cohort characteristics were summarized as frequencies and percentages for categorical variables, and as means with standard deviations or medians with interquartile ranges for continuous variables. Differences across the three cohorts were assessed using chi-square tests for categorical variables, and analysis of variance (ANOVA) or Kruskal–Wallis tests for continuous ones, as appropriate.

A logistic regression model was used to compare patients hospitalized with COVID-19 to those with other ARIs, adjusting for age, sex, and DDCI. As a result of the small size of the cohort hospitalized for the dual diagnosis of COVID-19 and other ARIs, these subjects were not included in the logistic models.
Results were reported as odds ratios (ORs) with 95% confidence intervals (CIs) and corresponding adjusted p values.

Given the varying mortality rates between groups, analyses were also repeated on the subset of patients who survived the entire follow-up period. A cumulative incidence function with death as a competing risk was plotted, and Gray’s test was used to assess statistical significance. To account for the multiple comparisons and control the false discovery rate (FDR), the Benjamini–Hochberg method was implemented. All statistical analyses were conducted using SAS software (SAS Institute Inc., Cary, NC, USA).

sec.title: Results
sec.p:
During 2021, a total of 74,181 individuals aged more than 40 years were hospitalized in Lombardy for ARI. After in-hospital deaths and residents of nursing homes were excluded, 57,795 patients were included in the final analysis: 35,458 with a hospitalization for COVID-19, 21,375 for other ARIs, and 962 for the dual diagnosis of COVID-19 and another ARI during the same hospitalization (Fig. 1).Fig. 1
Selection of study cohorts: inclusion and exclusion criteria

Patients’ characteristics are shown in Table 1.Table 1
Characteristics of patients

Female14,922 (42.1%)9222 (43.1%)360 (37.4%)

Hospitalization duration

Life status at 12 months after hospital discharge< 0.0001

Diabetes3404 (9.6%)2379 (11.1%)106 (11.0%)< 0.001
Heart diseases3406 (9.6%)3993 (18.7%)124 (12.9%)< 0.001
Liver disease396 (1.1%)414 (1.9%)15 (1.6%)< 0.001
Lung disease1654 (4.7%)3020 (14.1%)81 (8.4%)< 0.001
Renal failure878 (2.5%)1328 (6.2%)42 (4.4%)< 0.001
Venous thromboembolism399 (1.1%)509 (2.4%)11 (1.1%)< 0.001
DDCI Drug-Derived Complexity Index
ap value: chi-square for categorical variables and ANOVA or Kruskal–Wallis test for continuous ones

The COVID-19 cohort was the youngest (mean age 68.5 years) while other ARIs was the oldest (76.7 years). Similarly annual mortality was at its lowest for the COVID-19 cohort (10.7%) and at its highest for other ARIs (33.5%).
The length of stay was similar between COVID-19 and other ARI groups (median 12 days), but notably longer in those with both COVID-19 and another ARI (median 21 days).

For a detailed breakdown of characteristics specific to patients alive after 12 months (comprising 31,679 with COVID-19, 14,210 with other ARIs, and 775 with dual diagnosis), refer to Supplementary Table 3, which provides similar demographic and clinical data specifically for these sub-cohorts of survivors.

Regarding the use of specialist visits within 12 months after discharge, 75.8% of patients with COVID-19 received at least one specialist consultation, similarly to the dual-diagnosis group (75.7%) but higher than the ARI group (70.3%) (p < 0.0001). The majority of specialist follow-ups occurred in pneumology, followed by cardiology department and neurology (Supplementary Table 6). Moreover, the median time to the first specialist visit was longer for patients with COVID-19 (63 days) compared to other patients with ARI (45 days) (p < 0.0001).

When the competing risk of death was accounted for, the cumulative incidence of specialist visits after discharge showed a distinct temporal pattern: during the first 120 days, the other ARI group had more (early) visits, while after 120 days, the COVID-19 group surpassed other groups in cumulative incidence (Fig. 2). A further analysis of the cumulative incidence of specialist visits with competing risk of death, stratified by age, is illustrated in Supplementary Fig. 1.Fig. 2
Cumulative incidence of specialist visits from discharge to 365 days after, with competing risk of death

The COVID-19 cohort exhibited a higher rate of rehospitalization for COVID-19-related causes (7.7%) compared to the other ARI group (4.0%) (Table 2). Conversely, they showed lower rates of rehospitalization for other ARIs (2.4% vs. 10.6%) and for other non-ARI causes (19.3% vs. 28.7%) when compared to the other ARI group. The cumulative incidence of non-acute respiratory infection hospitalizations, accounting for the competing risk of death, is presented in Supplementary Fig. 2.Table 2
Patients subsequent use of health services, by hospitalization type

ap value: chi-square for categorical variables and ANOVA or Kruskal–Wallis test for continuous ones with false discovery rate adjusted

Moreover, patients with COVID-19 underwent more imaging tests such as chest CT scan (28.3% vs. 19.8%) and spirometry (18.6% vs. 8.0%) post-discharge than those with other ARIs. Laboratory testing rates were similar across groups, though glycemia, and liver enzyme testing were more frequent in the COVID-19 group.

For a detailed analysis of incident chronic drug exposure, focusing on patients alive at 12 months, Supplementary Table 5 provides comprehensive adjusted ORs and 95% CIs where only drugs for diabetes remained statistically significant.

Specifically, the prevalence of pre-existing diabetes was higher in the non-COVID ARI group (11.1%) compared to the COVID-19 group (9.6%). At the same time, a greater number of new diabetes cases were found in the COVID-19 group (2.6%) compared to the non-COVID ARI group (1.4%).

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: This study provided a comprehensive comparison of post-discharge healthcare services use among individuals hospitalized with COVID-19 versus other ARIs, using comprehensive administrative health data from Lombardy, Northern Italy.

According to our results, patients with COVID-19 were notably younger, had fewer comorbidities, and showed substantially lower 12-month post-discharge mortality compared to patients with other ARIs. This may reflect both differences in baseline susceptibility and acute disease selection. Specifically, COVID-19 mortality in hospital settings disproportionately affected the most vulnerable patients during hospitalization [13], whereas those surviving the hospitalization may represent a healthier subset. In contrast, other ARIs notoriously affect frail and older adult individuals, explaining the significantly higher post-discharge mortality observed in this group [14].

A key result of our analysis was the complex temporal pattern in specialist consultation rates. In fact, we found that COVID-19 survivors accessed specialist consultations at a slightly higher overall rate compared to patients with other ARIs. However, their utilization of healthcare assistance was characterized by a longer time to first visit, a lower incidence of specialist visits in the early post-discharge period, and a higher cumulative incidence beyond 120 days. This might mean that COVID-19 survivors were more likely to develop sequelae in the late phase of post-discharge recovery. This trend might be due to the younger age and lower underlying frailty of the COVID-19 group, who might have delayed the urgency of specialist follow-up. Moreover, system-level constraints during the pandemic may have contributed. Specifically, several Italian regions, including Lombardy, implemented strict infection control protocols in outpatient settings, including dedicated pathways for patients with COVID-19, extended time slots for disinfection of diagnostic equipment, and reduced clinic capacity due to social distancing requirements [15]. Similarly, recent studies have shown that fear of reinfection, coupled with the traumatic experience of hospitalization, led many individuals to delay or avoid further healthcare encounters after COVID-19-related hospitalization and discharge, even when symptoms persisted [16].

Furthermore, the increased incidence of visits beyond 120 days aligns with known post-acute sequelae of COVID-19, including new or persistent respiratory, cognitive, or metabolic symptoms [17, 18]. In fact, several studies reported that symptoms such as dyspnea, fatigue, or neurocognitive impairment frequently emerge or worsen months after acute illness [6, 19]. This could also explain the delayed healthcare engagement pattern. However, regarding the pulmonary function, there were higher rates of diagnostic chest imaging in COVID-19 survivors, and this raises concerns regarding residual interstitial lung disease and persistent functional impairment in these patients. Previous studies documented radiological abnormalities and impaired pulmonary capacity in COVID-19 survivors at 3–6 months post-discharge [20]. Despite overall lower rates of chronic medication initiation, patients with COVID-19 were significantly more likely to start treatment with antidiabetic agents and psychoanaleptics therapies (even though psychoanaleptics were not significant after subtracting patients deceased in the year following discharge), reinforcing the already known increased risk of new-onset diabetes mellitus [6] and mental health conditions [21] following acute SARS-CoV-2 infection.

Specifically, despite the prevalence of pre-existing diabetes and mental health disorders being higher in the non-COVID-19 ARI group, in our results a greater number of new diabetes cases and new prescriptions of psychoanaleptic drugs were found in the COVID-19 group. This finding highlights the link between SARS-CoV-2 infection and the development of metabolic disorders [22], and depression, anxiety, and post-traumatic stress disorders [23], even in individuals without a prior history of these diseases. These findings raise the hypothesis that, despite concern over “long COVID”, post-COVID syndrome may drive fewer systemic medical prescriptions than the long-term complications following other ARIs in elderly patients. In fact, while several population-based analyses have shown greater resource use and lingering complications among COVID-19 survivors [1, 8], most of the studies lacked non-COVID-19 controls. In contrast, our design used historical and concurrent ARI comparators from the same health system, and offered a more balanced real-world perspective: while COVID-19 generates specific long-term needs, especially in endocrinology and mental health, it does not necessarily impose a uniformly higher healthcare burden across all domains when compared with other non-COVID-19 ARIs.

Key strengths of this study include the use of high-quality, population-level administrative data and the inclusion of well-defined comparator groups. The large sample size and adjustment for age, sex, and baseline comorbidity enhance the internal validity of our findings.

However, limitations include the reliance on administrative codes, which may miss important clinical details such as subjects’ weight and BMI, and the lack of private or informal services data that potentially underestimated some healthcare utilization.

Finally, one major limitation to this study is the generalizability of these findings. Healthcare utilization may in part be a function of disease severity, because COVID-19 in 2021 was a substantially different and much more severe syndrome than in 2025.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Post-discharge healthcare utilization among COVID-19 survivors significantly differs from that of patients hospitalized with other ARIs. Our data show that while patients with COVID-19 were younger and healthier at baseline with lower mortality, they faced a delayed onset of specialist consultations, though their cumulative visit rate surpassed other ARI groups after 120 days. Importantly, these patients also demonstrated specific long-term needs, evidenced by higher rates of antidiabetic and psychoanaleptic drug initiations, as well as increased use of diagnostic chest imaging and spirometry. These patterns underscore the unique and delayed healthcare burdens associated with “long COVID.”

sec.title: Supplementary Information
sec.p:
Below is the link to the electronic supplementary material.
Supplementary file1 (PDF 539 KB)

fn.p: Springer Nature remains neutral with regard to jurisdictional claims in published maps and institutional affiliations.
Alessandra Bandera and Alessandro Nobili shared last authorship.

sec.title: Medical Writing, Editorial and Other Assistance
sec.p: We thank Giuseppe Preziosi, Monica Arivetti, and Giovanna Rigotti from ARIA S.p.A., Alfredo Bevilacqua from Laife Reply S.p.A., Marco D. Forlani from T Bridge—BV Tech S.p.A., and Igor Monti from Istituto di Ricerche Farmacologiche Mario Negri IRCCS who kindly assisted us with data collection. We also thank Ida Fortino, Olivia Leoni and Danilo Cereda of Directorate General for Health, Lombardy Region, 00144 Milan, Italy, for “Accesso al Patrimonio informativo regionale tramite DaaS 2.0—Emergenza Covid-19” (Parere “Commissione Tecnico Scientifica accesso dati Covid-19” (ex DDG n. 4564 del 15.04.2020) della seduta del 21/07/2020) and for revision of the manuscript.

notes.title: Author Contributions
notes.p: Alessandra Bandera: Conceptualization, funding acquisition, supervision, writing—review & editing. Marta Colaneri: Conceptualization, writing—original draft. Alessia A. Galbussera: Software, formal analysis, data curation, methodology, visualization. Alessandro Nobili: Funding acquisition, supervision, writing—review & editing. Massimo Puoti: Funding acquisition, writing—review & editing. Giulia Marchetti: Funding acquisition, writing—review & editing. Simone Piva: Funding acquisition, writing—review & editing. Pierluigi Plebani: Funding acquisition, writing—review & editing. Mario Raviglione: Supervision, writing—review & editing. Andrea Gori: Supervision, writing—review & editing. Mauro Tettamanti: Methodology, funding acquisition, supervision, writing—review & editing.

notes.title: Funding
notes.p: The study was funded by Fondazione Cariplo, grant nr. 2021-4236, “The Lombardia long COVID Network for the implementation of best practices in the comprehensive management of long COVID (LLC-Network)”. The funder had no role in the design, data collection, data analysis, and reporting of this study. Also, the Rapid Service Fee was funded by this grant.

notes.title: Data Availability
notes.p: Data that support the findings of this study were provided by the Directorate General for Health, Lombardy Region, but restrictions apply to the availability of these data and codes, which were used under license for the current study, and so they are not publicly available. Data are, however, available from the Directorate General for Health, Lombardy Region upon reasonable request.

notes.title: Conflict of Interest
notes.p: Alessandra Bandera, Giulia Marchetti received a grant from Gilead for data publication. Alessandra Bandera reports honoraria and payments from AstraZeneca-Biomeriux, Qiagen-Jassen Cilag, and Nordic-Pharma for lectures, presentations, speakers bureaus, manuscript writing, and educational events. Alessandra Bandera has received support for attending meetings and travel from Pfizer-AstraZeneca. Additionally, Alessandra Bandera, Giulia Marchetti participated on Data Safety Monitoring Boards or Advisory Boards for ViiV, Sobi, and Gilead. Pierluigi Plebani has received grants from the European Commission (Horizon Europe Project TEADAL, grant agreement no. 101070186), the Italian Ministry of Health (Health Big Data Project), and the MADE Competence Center for the supervision of the Data Analytics and Cybersecurity area in MADE. Pierluigi Plebani reports honoraria as the Director of the Masters in Cloud and Data Engineering at Cefriel for lectures, presentations, manuscript writing, and educational events. Pierluigi Plebani has provided expert testimony for ATM Spa related to the analysis of their information systems. Additionally, Pierluigi Plebani has held a leadership role as Chair of IFIP WG 2.14/6.12/8.10 from 2017 to 2023. Marta Colaneri, Alessia A. Galbussera, Mauro Tettamanti, Massimo Puoti, Simone Piva, Mario Raviglione, Andrea Gori, and Alessandro Nobili has nothing to disclose.

notes.title: Ethical Approval
notes.p: According to Italian law, studies using retrospective aggregated data from administrative databases that do not involve direct access by investigators to individual patient data do not require approval, notification from an ethics committee/institutional review board, or patient informed consent. Data access was granted through an agreement between the Istituto di Ricerche Farmacologiche Mario Negri IRCCS and the Lombardy Welfare Directorate. All data were not identifiable and were handled in compliance with Italian privacy regulations.
